passages.explanation: The disciples come to Jesus: who is greatest in the Kingdom of Heaven? Jesus calls a little child, sets him in their midst, and says: unless you turn and become like little children, you will never enter the Kingdom of Heaven. Whoever humbles himself like this little child is greatest in the kingdom. Whoever receives one such little child in my name receives me. But whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to stumble — it would be better for him that a huge millstone were hung around his neck and he were drowned in the depths of the sea. The disciples ask about status. Jesus answers with a child — someone with no status at all. The kingdom inverts the hierarchy.

Woe to the world because of occasions of stumbling! It's necessary that the occasions come, but woe to the person through whom they come. If your hand or foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It's better to enter into life maimed than to be cast into eternal fire with two hands or feet. If your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out — better to enter life with one eye than to be cast into the Gehenna of fire with two eyes. This echoes the Sermon on the Mount but now applies the radical surgery language to causing others to stumble. The severity matches the stakes.

See that you don't despise one of these little ones, for I tell you that their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father. What do you think? If a man has a hundred sheep and one goes astray, doesn't he leave the ninety-nine on the mountains and go search for the one? If he finds it, he rejoices over it more than over the ninety-nine that didn't go astray. Even so, it's not the will of your Father in heaven that one of these little ones should perish. God's maths doesn't work like ours. The one who wanders matters as much as the ninety-nine who stay.

If your brother sins against you, go and show him his fault between you and him alone. If he listens, you've gained your brother. If he doesn't listen, take one or two more with you, so that every word may be established by two or three witnesses. If he refuses to listen to them, tell it to the assembly. If he refuses to listen even to the assembly, let him be to you as a Gentile or a tax collector. Whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven; whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven. Where two or three are gathered together in my name, there I am in their midst. Jesus gives a clear process: private first, then small group, then community, then separation. Every step tries to restore before escalating. The goal is reconciliation, not punishment.

Peter comes and asks: Lord, how often should I forgive my brother who sins against me? Up to seven times? Jesus says: not seven times, but seventy times seven. The Kingdom of Heaven is like a king who settles accounts with his servants. One owes ten thousand talents — an astronomical, unpayable debt. He can't pay, and the king orders him sold with his family and possessions. The servant falls down: have patience with me, I'll pay everything. The king is moved with compassion, releases him, and forgives the entire debt. That servant goes out and finds a fellow servant who owes him a hundred denarii — a trivial amount by comparison. He grabs him by the throat: pay what you owe! The fellow servant begs for patience, but the first servant throws him in prison. Other servants see this, are grieved, and report to the king. The king summons him: you wicked servant! I forgave you all that debt because you begged me. Shouldn't you have had mercy, as I had mercy on you? The king delivers him to the tormentors until he pays everything owed. So my heavenly Father will also do to you, if you don't each forgive your brother from your hearts. The parable's force is in the ratio — ten thousand talents versus a hundred denarii. If you understand how much you've been forgiven, unforgiveness becomes absurd.
